caveats: a: O*NET covers property managers across residential and commercial assets rather than self-storage employees.
a: The task sources do not provide self-storage task-time or wage shares.
a: Public Storage is a technology leader, so its current automation baseline may exceed that of lower-middle-market operators.
rho: Digital rental completion is not itself AI implementation.
rho: A large public REIT is not representative of independent lower-middle-market technology budgets.
rho: Lien, eviction, access, and consumer-protection rules create state-specific exception work.
e: Facility, establishment, property-owning entity, and operating company counts can differ materially.
e: The supplied firm count is margin-bridged and may be distorted by real-estate ownership structures.
e: Public-company square-foot ownership does not measure the share of firms eligible in the supplied band.

s5: Portfolio transactions can include many facilities but only one control transfer.
s5: No observed owner-age or succession distribution is available for the target population.

q: Local supply and customer-acquisition spending can dissipate labor savings.
q: No source directly observes gross-benefit pass-through after automation.
d5: Revenue and occupancy do not directly measure constant-price, constant-quality service quantity.
d5: Public portfolios may differ from lower-middle-market facilities by geography, quality, and marketing scale.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Lessors of Miniwarehouses and Self-Storage Units

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Households and businesses usually pay recurring unit rent, often month to month, with ancillary administrative fees, merchandise, and tenant-protection or insurance revenue.
lens.excluded_activities: Public and contract warehousing, coin- or card-operated lockers, passive shells, captive internal storage, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the lower-middle-market band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market owner-operators that repeatedly rent secure rooms, compartments, containers, lockers, or outdoor space for customers to store and retrieve their own goods.

a.bridge: O*NET's property-manager task list is mapped to self-storage payroll: leasing and marketing, rate review, customer communication, collections, records, budgeting, complaint handling, and contractor coordination are treated as substantially AI-exposed, while inspections, security incidents, cleaning, repairs, and accountable lien or access decisions are not. Public Storage's already-digital rental workflow is used to remove work that is no longer manual. The broad occupation likely biases exposure upward because self-storage is leaner and more physically oriented than many managed properties.
caveats: O*NET covers property managers across residential and commercial assets rather than self-storage employees.
The task sources do not provide self-storage task-time or wage shares.
Public Storage is a technology leader, so its current automation baseline may exceed that of lower-middle-market operators.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time study across independent self-storage facilities separating already automated leasing, call-center, pricing, collections, security, inspection, maintenance, and lien-sale work.
a.rationale: A large share of remaining office work is structured, repetitive, and text- or data-heavy, but the industry's low staffing model and extensive existing digital rental adoption mean much of the easiest labor has already been removed.

d5.bridge: Public Storage's 2025 same-store revenue was roughly flat as softer demand reduced average occupancy, while National Storage Affiliates reported 93.0% same-store occupancy and 1.4% annual same-store revenue growth. Those public-portfolio operating observations are converted into a nearly flat five-year constant-quality storage-quantity base with room for household mobility, business use, new supply, and local saturation. Public REIT assets are typically larger and in denser markets, so applying their occupancy resilience to smaller operators likely biases demand modestly upward.
caveats: Revenue and occupancy do not directly measure constant-price, constant-quality service quantity.
Public portfolios may differ from lower-middle-market facilities by geography, quality, and marketing scale.
Demand is local and can diverge sharply with migration, housing turnover, and new construction.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year same-store occupied-square-foot and move-in data for independent facilities from Yardi Matrix, Radius+, or self-storage software vendors, segmented by local supply additions and market density.
d5.rationale: Customers retain a recurring need for flexible physical space, but current demand is soft in some markets and new construction can pressure mature facilities. The base therefore assumes limited net quantity growth rather than extrapolating portfolio expansion or nominal rent growth.

caveats: Facility, establishment, property-owning entity, and operating company counts can differ materially.
The supplied firm count is margin-bridged and may be distorted by real-estate ownership structures.
Public-company square-foot ownership does not measure the share of firms eligible in the supplied band.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census matching 531130 legal entities to facilities, normalized EBITDA, operating control, third-party management, customer contracts, and passive-property ownership.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition closely matches the recurring external-rental lens, and Public Storage describes most US square footage as owned by regional and local operators. Ineligible firms are mainly passive property entities, non-control vehicles, businesses without transferable operations, and records whose economics fall outside the target band.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 531130 ($3,272,192,371) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 531130 ($20,848,590,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0396 (margins.json[53]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Real Estate (Operations & Services) EBITDA/Sales 3.96% (agency/brokerage/property-management economics, dominant in firm counts)); revenue/employee $443,059 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 531130); class <5: 7,530 firms, avg revenue/firm $590,631 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $23,389 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 868 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,940,957 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $76,862 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 221 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,529,471 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $139,767 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 129 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,172,140 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $323,617 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 43 firms, avg revenue/firm $49,476,651 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,959,275 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $488,848,682 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $19,358,408 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 43

caveats: Operator requirement varies between simple drive-up sites and climate-controlled multistory facilities.
Remote management may shift work to centralized or outsourced staff rather than eliminate it.
Future access-control and computer-vision systems could reduce the accountable human share further.
o.change_evidence: A cohort of remotely operated and conventionally staffed facilities measuring operator labor, outsourced exceptions, security losses, legal events, maintenance response, retention, and fully autonomous operating hours.
o.rationale: Digital rental, payment, access, and account service can make a facility largely unmanned, but secure physical space still requires an accountable operator for access control, surveillance response, repairs, cleanliness, insurance, property compliance, delinquency, and lien procedures. Software can reduce staffing without replacing ownership and operational responsibility for the site.

caveats: Public REIT pricing systems and brand advantages may overstate independent operators' retention.
Local supply and customer-acquisition spending can dissipate labor savings.
No source directly observes gross-benefit pass-through after automation.
q.change_evidence: Facility cohorts comparing post-automation payroll savings, advertised and realized rents, discounts, churn, occupancy, and renewal increases against matched non-adopters.
q.rationale: Monthly unit rents are priced for space and access rather than labor hours, so automation savings can initially remain with the operator. Centralized rate management and existing-tenant increases support capture, while transparent online prices, local oversupply, promotions, and lower move-in rates pass part of the benefit to customers.

rho.bridge: Public Storage reports that nearly three quarters of new rental agreements used eRental or Rent by Phone in 2025 and describes centralized pricing, marketing, access, payment, and account-management systems. Those demonstrated digital workflows are translated into the five-year implementable share of the remaining AI-exposed work at lower-middle-market operators. The public-company platform is better resourced than the target population, so this proxy likely biases implementation upward.
caveats: Digital rental completion is not itself AI implementation.
A large public REIT is not representative of independent lower-middle-market technology budgets.
Lien, eviction, access, and consumer-protection rules create state-specific exception work.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor telemetry from self-storage management platforms showing production AI adoption, automated-contact resolution, delinquency workflows, pricing overrides, and labor hours removed by facility size.
rho.rationale: Standard facility-management software, remote access, digital payments, call routing, and centralized pricing make integration feasible, but fragmented legacy systems, site-level exceptions, legal process variation, cybersecurity, and small-operator capital constraints slow full implementation.

caveats: Public Storage's acquisitions are not an industry-wide deal census and skew toward institutionally suitable assets.
Portfolio transactions can include many facilities but only one control transfer.
No observed owner-age or succession distribution is available for the target population.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year facility-level transaction database from Green Street, Yardi Matrix, Radius+, or major self-storage brokers, deduplicated to selling firms and matched to the eligible lower-middle-market denominator and owner age.
s5.rationale: The sector is fragmented and active strategic buyers exist: Public Storage acquired 87 facilities in 2025 and 273 since the start of 2023. Those transactions establish liquidity but not a transfer probability for eligible independent firms, so the base remains a bounded judgment.

principal_driver: Recurring fixed-price rent and standardized digital workflows create a practical route to retain some centralized operating efficiencies.
principal_weakness: The business is already labor-light and digitally self-served, leaving a limited unautomated wage base while local supply can absorb savings through price competition.
